subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalaamu alaykum, young scientist! In this unit you are going to become a rock detective. Everywhere you look, from the sandy shore of the Indian Ocean at Lamu to the tall red cliffs of the Rift Valley, the Earth is made of rocks. Rocks are under the soil in your garden, under the road you walk on, and even inside the walls of many houses. When you finish this unit, you will never look at a stone the same way again.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Identify and describe different rocks using their properties, such as colour, texture, hardness, and weight.
Explain how people use rocks to build houses, roads, and beautiful things.
Describe what soil is made of and how broken rock slowly becomes soil.
Sort materials into natural (made by nature) and man-made (made by people).
Observe and record the properties of materials carefully, the way a real scientist does.
Use what you know about rock properties to choose the best material for a job.
A hard, solid material that comes from the Earth, made by nature.
The grey stones in a wall in Hargeisa.
A word that describes what something is like - its colour, feel, hardness, or weight.

concepts.title: What Is a Rock?
concepts.explanation: A rock is a hard, solid material that is made by nature and found in the Earth. Let us think about what makes something a rock. First, a rock is hard and solid - it is not like water that pours or air that you cannot hold. Second, a rock is natural, which means nature made it, not people in a factory. Third, rocks come in many colours, sizes, and shapes. And fourth, rocks are made of tiny pieces called minerals that fit together like the pieces of a puzzle. Imagine you are walking along the beach at Kismaayo. You…
concepts.example: When scientists want to describe a rock, they look at its properties. A property is simply a word that tells us what the rock is like. Here are the properties you will learn to observe:

concepts.title: How People Use Rocks
concepts.explanation: People have used rocks for thousands of years, all over the world and right here in East Africa. Rocks are useful because of their properties. Let us see how the very same properties you learned to observe are exactly what make rocks so helpful. Why are rocks so good for building houses, walls, and roads? Think about their properties:
concepts.example: Hard rocks last a long time. They do not break easily in the sun, the rain, or the wind.

concepts.title: The Story of Soil
concepts.explanation: Now let us dig into one of the most important materials on the whole Earth: soil. Without soil, farmers near the Jubba River could not grow bariis, maize, or vegetables, the acacia and baobab trees could not stand tall, and there would be almost no food for people or animals. Soil is precious, and it has an amazing story. If you look very closely at a handful of soil, perhaps with a magnifying glass, you will see that it is not just one thing - it is a mixture of several things all together:
concepts.example: Tiny rock pieces: The biggest part of most soil. Big rocks slowly break into sand, then even smaller silt, then the tiniest pieces of all, called clay.

concepts.title: Natural and Man-Made Materials
concepts.explanation: Let us become material detectives. Everything around you is made of some kind of material - wood, stone, plastic, cotton, metal, glass. We can sort all materials into two big groups: natural materials and man-made materials. A natural material is made by nature, not by people. It can be found in the world around us and has not been changed very much. Natural materials come from three main places:
concepts.example: From plants: wood from trees, cotton for clothes, and the frankincense harvested from trees in Somalia.

explorations.title: The Rock Detective
explorations.context: To observe and describe the properties of different rocks, just like a real scientist.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you think: Will all your rocks feel and look the same, or will they be different? Write your guess. A guess you write before an experiment is called a hypothesis. What You Need 3 or 4 different stones from your garden, yard,…
explorations.answer: Run the investigation, record your observations, and compare them with your prediction.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe and describe the properties of different rocks, just like a real scientist.

explorations.title: How Hard Is Your Rock?
explorations.context: To test and rank rocks from softest to hardest using a simple hardness scale.
explorations.prompt: Look at your rocks. Which one do you think is the hardest? Write your guess before you test. What You Need 4 or 5 different rocks Your fingernail A coin A steel key or nail (an adult uses this one) A recording sheet What To Do Try to scratch each rock with…
explorations.answer: Run the investigation, record your observations, and compare them with your prediction.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To test and rank rocks from softest to hardest using a simple hardness scale.

explorations.title: The Soil Settling Jar
explorations.context: To separate soil into its parts and see what soil is really made of.
explorations.prompt: What do you think you will see inside the soil when it settles in water? Write your guess. What You Need A clear jar with a lid (a clean jam jar works well) Soil from a garden, farm, or pot - about one cup Water A ruler What To Do Put the soil into the jar…
explorations.answer: Run the investigation, record your observations, and compare them with your prediction.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To separate soil into its parts and see what soil is really made of.

explorations.title: Which Soil Drains Fastest?
explorations.context: To find out how quickly water drains through different kinds of soil.
explorations.prompt: Which do you think lets water through faster - sandy soil or sticky clay soil? Write your guess. What You Need 2 clear plastic bottles cut in half by an adult (use the top halves as funnels, turned upside down) 2 clean cups or jars to catch the water Two…
explorations.answer: Run the investigation, record your observations, and compare them with your prediction.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out how quickly water drains through different kinds of soil.

explorations.title: Natural or Man-Made?
explorations.context: To sort everyday objects into natural and man-made groups and trace where they came from.
explorations.prompt: Do you think most objects in your home are natural or man-made? Write your guess before you sort. What You Need 8 to 10 small safe objects from around your home (for example: a wooden spoon, a plastic cup, a cotton cloth, a stone, a metal spoon, a glass jar,…
explorations.answer: Run the investigation, record your observations, and compare them with your prediction.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To sort everyday objects into natural and man-made groups and trace where they came from.

workedExamples.title: Thinking Like a Scientist
workedExamples.prompt: When scientists measure and count, they show every step of their thinking. Let us work through two examples together so you can see how it is done.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: A Hardness Test Amina wants to find out how hard three of her rocks are. She uses three safe tests, from softest to hardest: her fingernail, a coin, and (with an adult helping) a steel key. If something can scratch the rock, the rock is softer than that thing. Here is what Amina found: Fingernail scratches it?

workedExamples.title: 1: A Hardness Test
workedExamples.prompt: Amina wants to find out how hard three of her rocks are. She uses three safe tests, from softest to hardest: her fingernail, a coin, and (with an adult helping) a steel key. If something can scratch the rock, the rock is softer than that thing. Here is what…
workedExamples.solution: Fingernail scratches it? Coin scratches it? Steel scratches it?

workedExamples.title: 2: Reading Soil Layers
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf shook some soil in a jar of water and let it settle. The heaviest pieces (sand) sank to the bottom, the middle pieces (silt) settled next, and the lightest pieces (clay) settled on top. He measured the layers with a ruler:
workedExamples.solution: Sand layer at the bottom: 6 cm Silt layer in the middle: 3 cm Clay layer on top: 1 cm Step 1: Add the layers together to find the total: 6 + 3 + 1 = 10 cm of soil. Step 2: Look at which layer is biggest. The sand layer (6 cm) is the largest, more than half of the whole 10 cm. Answer: Because there is so much sand, Yusuf has sandy soil. This tells him that water will drain through it quickly, so plants growing in it may need watering more often. See how measuring helped Yusuf understand his soil!

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Beach pebbles are smooth because: (a) people polish them (b) water rolls them for a long time (c) they are man-made (d) they are soft
workedExamples.solution: (b) water rolls them for a long time. Moving water wears away sharp edges over many years - this is weathering.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a natural material? (a) plastic (b) glass (c) wool (d) steel
workedExamples.solution: (c) wool. Wool comes from animals like sheep and goats, so it is natural.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a man-made material? (a) stone (b) plastic (c) cotton (d) wood
workedExamples.solution: (b) plastic. Plastic is made by people from oil, so it is man-made.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Soil is made of: (a) only sand (b) only water (c) tiny rock pieces, dead plants, air and water (d) only dead leaves
workedExamples.solution: (c) tiny rock pieces, dead plants, air and water. Soil is a mixture of all of these.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The slow breaking of rocks into smaller pieces is called: (a) building (b) weathering (c) melting (d) floating
workedExamples.solution: (b) weathering. Weathering is the slow breaking of rock into smaller pieces.

practice.prompt: A rock is a material that is: (a) made in a factory (b) hard and made by nature (c) always soft (d) alive
practice.answer: (b) hard and made by nature. A rock is a solid, natural material from the Earth - not made in a factory and not alive.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The way a rock feels when you touch it is called its: (a) colour (b) weight (c) texture (d) size
practice.answer: (c) texture. Texture is how a surface feels: smooth, rough, bumpy, or grainy.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Hardness means: (a) how heavy a rock is (b) how hard it is to scratch (c) how shiny it is (d) its colour
practice.answer: (b) how hard it is to scratch. Harder materials resist scratching; softer ones scratch easily.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Beach pebbles are smooth because: (a) people polish them (b) water rolls them for a long time (c) they are man-made (d) they are soft
practice.answer: (b) water rolls them for a long time. Moving water wears away sharp edges over many years - this is weathering.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a natural material? (a) plastic (b) glass (c) wool (d) steel
practice.answer: (c) wool. Wool comes from animals like sheep and goats, so it is natural.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a man-made material? (a) stone (b) plastic (c) cotton (d) wood
practice.answer: (b) plastic. Plastic is made by people from oil, so it is man-made.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Soil is made of: (a) only sand (b) only water (c) tiny rock pieces, dead plants, air and water (d) only dead leaves
practice.answer: (c) tiny rock pieces, dead plants, air and water. Soil is a mixture of all of these.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The slow breaking of rocks into smaller pieces is called: (a) building (b) weathering (c) melting (d) floating
practice.answer: (b) weathering. Weathering is the slow breaking of rock into smaller pieces.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Glass is made from: (a) wood (b) sand (c) wool (d) oil
practice.answer: (b) sand. Glass is made by melting sand at a very high heat.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The best soil for growing most plants is: (a) sandy soil (b) clay soil (c) loamy soil (d) no soil
practice.answer: (c) loamy soil. Loam is a balanced mix that holds water but also drains - best for most plants.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which rock property makes rocks good for building strong walls? (a) they are soft (b) they are hard and do not rot (c) they are light (d) they burn easily
practice.answer: (b) they are hard and do not rot. Hard, long-lasting rocks make strong walls.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Coral stone, used to build houses in Lamu, comes from: (a) a factory (b) the sea (c) trees (d) plastic
practice.answer: (b) the sea. Coral stone is a natural rock that forms in the sea.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Observe: How People Use Rocks
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of how people use rocks around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Rock
A hard, solid, natural material from the Earth.
Mineral
A natural substance that rocks are made from.
Property
A word that describes what something is like (colour, texture, hardness, weight).
Texture
How something feels: smooth, rough, bumpy, or grainy.
Hardness
How difficult it is to scratch or break something.
Weathering
The slow breaking of rocks into smaller pieces over a long time.
Soil
A mixture of tiny rock pieces, dead plants, air, and water that plants grow in.
Humus
The dark, rich part of soil made from rotted plants and animals.
Natural
Made by nature, not by people (stone, wood, wool).
Man-made
Made by people using tools and machines (plastic, glass, steel).
Concrete
A man-made building material of crushed rock, sand, cement, and water.

vocabulary.meaning: A hard, solid, natural material from the Earth.
vocabulary.example: In this unit you are going to become a rock detective.

vocabulary.meaning: A natural substance that rocks are made from.
vocabulary.example: Pencils write with a soft mineral called graphite.

vocabulary.meaning: A word that describes what something is like (colour, texture, hardness, weight).
vocabulary.example: A property is simply a word that tells us what the rock is like.

vocabulary.meaning: How something feels: smooth, rough, bumpy, or grainy.
vocabulary.example: Identify and describe different rocks using their properties, such as colour, texture, hardness, and weight.

vocabulary.meaning: How difficult it is to scratch or break something.
vocabulary.example: Identify and describe different rocks using their properties, such as colour, texture, hardness, and weight.

vocabulary.meaning: The slow breaking of rocks into smaller pieces over a long time.
vocabulary.example: Waves weathering a rough rock into a smooth pebble.

vocabulary.meaning: A mixture of tiny rock pieces, dead plants, air, and water that plants grow in.
vocabulary.example: Rocks, their properties, soil, and the materials all around you

vocabulary.meaning: The dark, rich part of soil made from rotted plants and animals.
vocabulary.example: Dark brown or black soil is usually rich in humus and very good for growing food.

vocabulary.meaning: Made by nature, not by people (stone, wood, wool).
vocabulary.example: Sort materials into natural (made by nature) and man-made (made by people).

vocabulary.meaning: Made by people using tools and machines (plastic, glass, steel).
vocabulary.example: Sort materials into natural (made by nature) and man-made (made by people).

vocabulary.meaning: A man-made building material of crushed rock, sand, cement, and water.
vocabulary.example: Concrete, which is used for floors, bridges, and modern buildings, is made by mixing crushed rock and sand with cement (which itself is made from ground-up limestone) and water.

commonMistakes: Man-made things do not come from nature.

connections.text: You will explore materials and their properties even more - floating, sinking, and letting water through.
Plants (Science): Soil connects to how plants get water and food through their roots.
Rocks and soil shape the land - mountains, beaches, rivers, and farms of East Africa.
You measure rock hardness and soil layers, and count and compare your results.
People carve and polish rocks and use natural clay to make beautiful things.

[Earth] Khalifah of the Earth In Islam we are taught to care for the land and not to waste. Protect soil, reuse man-made things, and keep water and land clean. Looking closely at rocks and soil is a way of reflecting on the wonders of…

realProblems.prompt: Use your science knowledge to answer these real-life questions.
realProblems.answer: Hassan should choose a hard rock. Hard rocks do not break or crumble, so the wall will be strong and last a long time.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Hassan should choose a hard rock. Hard rocks do not break or crumble, so the wall will be strong and last a long time.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan wants to build a strong garden wall in Hargeisa. Should he choose a hard rock or a soft, crumbly rock? Explain why.
realProblems.answer: Her soil is most likely sandy, because sandy soil drains fast. She could add compost or organic matter (humus) to help it hold water.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Her soil is most likely sandy, because sandy soil drains fast. She could add compost or organic matter (humus) to help it hold water.

realProblems.prompt: Amina's plant pot has soil that dries out very fast after she waters it. Is her soil most likely sandy or clay? What could she add to help it hold water?
realProblems.answer: The beach stone was weathered smooth by the ocean waves rolling it for years; the hillside stone had little water action, so it stayed rough and sharp.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The beach stone was weathered smooth by the ocean waves rolling it for years; the hillside stone had little water action, so it stayed rough and sharp.

realProblems.prompt: Khadija finds a smooth, round grey stone at the beach in Kismaayo and a rough, sharp stone on a hillside. Explain why they are so different.
realProblems.answer: Yusuf should use the plastic container, because plastic is waterproof and will not leak, while a woven basket lets water through.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Yusuf should use the plastic container, because plastic is waterproof and will not leak, while a woven basket lets water through.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf needs to carry water home from the well without it leaking. Should he use a woven basket or a plastic container? Explain your choice.
realProblems.answer: Soil (the topsoil) is being washed away. This is a problem because soil takes hundreds of years to form and plants and farmers need it to grow food.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Soil (the topsoil) is being washed away. This is a problem because soil takes hundreds of years to form and plants and farmers need it to grow food.

realProblems.prompt: During the heavy rains, Halima sees muddy brown water rushing down a bare slope. Explain what is being washed away and why that is a problem.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why rocks are good for building houses.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Any three of: colour, texture, hardness, weight, size, shape. (For example: grey, smooth, heavy.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name two hidden rocks - everyday things that are made from rock.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Rocks are hard, strong, do not rot, and do not burn easily, so they last a long time and make safe, sturdy buildings.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: List the main things that soil is made of.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Any two of: toothpaste, glass, salt, pencil (graphite), chalk, metal spoons/pans. These are made from rocks or minerals.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a natural material and a man-made material?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Soil is made of tiny rock pieces, dead plants and animals (humus), air, water, and living things like worms.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three kinds of soil.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Natural materials are made by nature (wood, wool, stone); man-made materials are made by people using tools and machines (plastic, glass, steel).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does a beach pebble feel smoother than a mountain rock?

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can identify and describe different rocks using their properties, such as colour, texture, hardness, and weight.
I can explain how people use rocks to build houses, roads, and beautiful things.
I can describe what soil is made of and how broken rock slowly becomes soil.
I can sort materials into natural (made by nature) and man-made (made by people).
I can observe and record the properties of materials carefully, the way a real scientist does.
I can use what you know about rock properties to choose the best material for a job.
I can a hard, solid material that comes from the Earth, made by nature.
